prompt:
You are reviewing a radar snapshot at 14:00:00 UTC. Two IFR aircraft are in en-route (Class A) airspace:

Aircraft 1 — AAL101: Position 40.500°N 073.800°W, Altitude FL350, Heading 090, Speed 450 kts
Aircraft 2 — UAL202: Position 40.500°N 073.170°W, Altitude FL350, Heading 270, Speed 420 kts

Both aircraft are on reciprocal headings (090 vs 270) at the same latitude and same altitude.

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both aircraft flying straight-line tracks at constant altitude and speed.

Identify all hazards present in this snapshot.
gold_decision:
THREE hazards are present:
1. HORIZONTAL SEPARATION VIOLATION (Critical): Both aircraft are at 40.500°N. AAL101 is at 073.800°W, UAL202 at 073.170°W. Longitude difference = 0.630°. At latitude 40.5°N, 1° longitude ≈ 0.766 × 60 NM = approximately 45.96 NM per degree, so 0.630° ≈ 28.96 NM. WAIT — this is the CURRENT separation. Current horizontal separation ≈ 29 NM. This is above the 5 NM minimum currently. HOWEVER:
2. CLOSING RATE HAZARD (Critical): AAL101 heading 090 (east) at 450 kts + UAL202 heading 270 (west) at 420 kts = closing rate 870 kts. Time to 5 NM separation: (29 NM - 5 NM) / 870 kts = 24/870 hours = 1.655 min. They will reach 5 NM separation in approximately 1.7 minutes and reach 0 NM (collision point) in 29/870 hours = 2.0 minutes.
3. ZERO VERTICAL SEPARATION: Both at FL350 — no vertical separation whatsoever. With no vertical separation and a closing rate of 870 kts, this is an imminent loss-of-separation and TCAS RA scenario. Controller must IMMEDIATELY issue crossing restrictions or altitude change to one aircraft.
required_safety_constraints: Reciprocal headings with zero vertical separation is an imminent collision scenario — controller must act immediately
Time to 5 NM separation at 870 kt closing rate: approximately 1.7 minutes — within CA alert threshold
Safety alert must be issued to both aircraft immediately per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-6
Altitude or heading change must be issued immediately — vectoring one aircraft 90° or assigning altitude separation are the fastest resolutions
acceptable_variants: Exact haversine distance calculation may yield a slightly different NM value — the key hazard identification is the reciprocal headings + same altitude + high closing rate
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 — En-route 5 NM separation minimum
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-6 — Safety alert requirements
Closing rate calculation: 450 + 420 = 870 kts for reciprocal tracks

provenance.generation_rule: Two aircraft on reciprocal headings (090 vs 270) at same latitude 40.500°N, same altitude FL350. Longitudes 073.800°W and 073.170°W = 0.630° separation. At 40.5°N cos(40.5°)=0.7604; 0.630° × 60 NM × 0.7604 ≈ 28.74 NM current separation. Closing rate 870 kts; time to 5 NM: (28.74-5)/870 × 60 = 1.64 min. Imminent loss-of-separation scenario. Generation rule: straight-line constant-altitude tracks from stated positions/headings/speeds.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 10:30:00 UTC. Terminal airspace (Class B, below FL180). Three aircraft:

Aircraft 1 — SWA334: Position 33.940°N 118.410°W, Altitude 8,000 ft, Heading 270, Speed 210 kts (inbound ILS 25L)
Aircraft 2 — DAL445: Position 33.940°N 118.620°W, Altitude 8,000 ft, Heading 270, Speed 180 kts (inbound ILS 25L, same final approach course)
Aircraft 3 — SKW556: Position 33.942°N 118.208°W, Altitude 10,000 ft, Heading 260, Speed 220 kts (inbound ILS 25R, parallel runway, 4,200 ft centerline spacing)

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): All three aircraft flying straight-line constant-altitude approaches.

Identify all hazards and separation concerns in this snapshot.
gold_decision:
HAZARDS IDENTIFIED:
1. SWA334 and DAL445 — Same final course (ILS 25L), same altitude 8,000 ft:
   Longitude difference: 118.620°W - 118.410°W = 0.210°. At 33.940°N, cos(33.940°)=0.8302; 0.210° × 60 × 0.8302 ≈ 10.46 NM separation. Current separation 10.46 NM. SWA334 is AHEAD of DAL445 (DAL is further west/behind). Closing rate: 210 kts (SWA) vs 180 kts (DAL) — SWA is faster so separation is INCREASING. No current hazard for this pair.
2. SWA334 and SKW556 — Different altitudes (8,000 ft vs 10,000 ft) = 2,000 ft vertical separation. Terminal 3 NM horizontal is the standard. Checking lateral: SKW556 is on parallel approach (25R, 4,200 ft centerline spacing ≈ 0.69 NM). Vertical separation 2,000 ft ≥ 1,000 ft. No hazard.
3. DAL445 HAZARD — DAL445 is behind SWA334 by 10.46 NM and slower (180 vs 210 kts) — separation is increasing. However if SWA334 slows for approach, closing could develop. Monitor required.
OVERALL: No immediate loss-of-separation. One latent hazard: if SWA334 decelerates to final approach speed and DAL445 fails to reduce speed, the in-trail spacing could compress. Controller should ensure speed sequencing is active.
required_safety_constraints: Terminal 3 NM separation standard applies in Class B below FL180 per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4b
In-trail arrivals on same ILS: monitor for compression if lead aircraft decelerates
Parallel approach 25L/25R: 4,200 ft centerline spacing qualifies for simultaneous independent approaches without PRM per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-6 — NTZ must be monitored
acceptable_variants: Exact NM distances require haversine calculation at the given latitude; the key hazard finding is the in-trail pair and parallel approach monitoring requirement
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4b — Terminal 3 NM separation
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-6 — Simultaneous independent approaches (4,300 ft standard; 4,200 ft is marginal)

provenance.generation_rule: Three-aircraft terminal snapshot. SWA334 and DAL445 on same final 25L at same altitude — SWA ahead by 0.210° lon ≈ 10.46 NM at 33.940°N. SWA faster (210 vs 180 kt), separation increasing — no immediate hazard but latent risk. SKW556 on parallel 25R at 10,000 ft — 2000 ft vertical clearance. Parallel approach spacing 4,200 ft. Generation rule: straight-line constant-altitude approach tracks from given positions.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 08:15:00 UTC. En-route Class A airspace.

Aircraft 1 — FedEx1: Position 35.000°N 090.000°W, Altitude FL310, Heading 090, Speed 480 kts
Aircraft 2 — FedEx2: Position 35.000°N 089.620°W, Altitude FL290, Heading 090, Speed 450 kts

Both aircraft on same eastbound track. FedEx1 is behind FedEx2 (same heading, FedEx1 is further west).

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both flying straight-line constant-altitude tracks eastbound.

Identify hazards. Calculate the current in-trail separation and state whether it is sufficient.
gold_decision:
ANALYSIS:
Current positions: FedEx1 at 090.000°W, FedEx2 at 089.620°W. FedEx1 is behind (further west) on same eastbound track. Longitude gap = 090.000°W - 089.620°W = 0.380° of longitude. At 35.000°N, cos(35°) = 0.8192; 0.380° × 60 NM × 0.8192 = 18.68 NM in-trail separation.

FedEx1 speed: 480 kts (behind). FedEx2 speed: 450 kts (ahead). Closing rate: 480 - 450 = 30 kts. FedEx1 is CATCHING FedEx2.

Time to 5 NM separation: (18.68 - 5) / 30 = 13.68 / 30 = 0.456 hours = 27.4 minutes.

Vertical separation: FL310 (FedEx1) - FL290 (FedEx2) = 2,000 ft. This exceeds the 1,000 ft RVSM minimum — vertical separation IS maintained.

HAZARDS: No immediate loss-of-separation (vertical 2,000 ft ≥ 1,000 ft RVSM). However, IF FedEx1 descends to FL290, vertical separation is lost and the 5 NM en-route minimum would apply — at 18.68 NM current horizontal, that would be fine now but decreasing at 30 kts. The latent hazard is: if a descent is issued to FedEx1 to FL290, horizontal separation must be verified at 5 NM before the altitude transition.
required_safety_constraints: Vertical 2,000 ft separation is above 1,000 ft RVSM minimum — currently compliant
Closing rate 30 kts in-trail: horizontal separation will erode — if vertical removed, 5 NM must be maintained
Before issuing any altitude change to FedEx1 to FL290, confirm 5 NM horizontal separation will exist during the transition
acceptable_variants: Haversine for 0.380° at 35°N: exact value ≈ 18.68 NM; accept ±0.5 NM
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §4-6-1 — RVSM 1,000 ft vertical minimum (FL290-FL410)
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 — En-route 5 NM horizontal minimum
Closing rate = speed differential on same track

provenance.generation_rule: Two eastbound aircraft at FL310 and FL290. FedEx1 (480 kt) behind FedEx2 (450 kt) on same track. Lon gap 0.380° at 35°N = 18.68 NM. Closing rate 30 kt. Time to 5 NM: 27.4 min. Vertical 2000 ft = above 1000 ft RVSM. No immediate hazard; latent risk if altitude change issued. Generation rule: straight-line eastbound constant-altitude tracks from stated positions.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 16:00:00 UTC. En-route airspace over the Midwest.

Aircraft 1 — N12345 (Cessna 172, VFR): Position 41.000°N 088.000°W, Altitude 3,500 ft MSL, Heading 180, Speed 110 kts. Aircraft is in Class E airspace (uncontrolled for VFR).
Aircraft 2 — UAL500 (B737, IFR): Position 41.050°N 088.010°W, Altitude 4,000 ft MSL, Heading 180, Speed 250 kts

UAL500 is on an IFR clearance in Class E airspace. N12345 is a VFR aircraft in the same Class E airspace.

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both flying straight-line southbound constant-altitude tracks.

Identify all hazards and ATC separation responsibilities.
gold_decision:
HAZARDS:
1. VERTICAL SEPARATION: UAL500 at 4,000 ft, N12345 at 3,500 ft = 500 ft vertical separation. The standard IFR vertical separation minimum is 1,000 ft. 500 ft is BELOW the minimum.
2. HORIZONTAL SEPARATION: Positions are 0.050° lat and 0.010° lon apart. At 41°N: lat diff = 0.050° × 60 NM = 3.0 NM; lon diff = 0.010° × 60 × cos(41°) = 0.010 × 60 × 0.7547 = 0.45 NM. Total distance ≈ √(3.0² + 0.45²) ≈ 3.03 NM. Below 5 NM en-route minimum.
3. ATC RESPONSIBILITY: In Class E airspace, ATC is responsible for separating IFR from IFR. ATC does NOT provide separation between IFR and VFR in Class E — see-and-avoid applies. However, ATC must issue traffic advisories when workload permits. The vertical 500 ft with 3 NM lateral means if N12345 is not visible to UAL500 crew, this is a see-and-avoid failure risk. ATC should issue a traffic advisory to UAL500.
required_safety_constraints: Class E airspace: ATC separates IFR from IFR; VFR see-and-avoid applies for IFR-VFR encounters
500 ft vertical between IFR aircraft would be a loss-of-separation; here one aircraft is VFR so ATC separation does not apply — but the collision risk is real
Traffic advisory must be issued to UAL500 per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-22 when workload permits

The VFR aircraft at 3,500 ft MSL must be at odd thousands + 500 ft on eastbound headings per 14 CFR §91.159 — heading 180 (southbound) should be at even thousands + 500 ft: 3,500 ft is correct for southbound VFR
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-8-2 — Class E airspace ATC responsibilities
14 CFR §91.159 — VFR cruising altitudes
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-22 — Traffic advisories

provenance.generation_rule: IFR B737 and VFR C172 in Class E airspace, both southbound. Vertical 500 ft (4000 vs 3500 ft). Horizontal: 0.050° lat × 60 = 3 NM, 0.010° lon × 60 × cos(41°) = 0.45 NM, total ≈ 3.03 NM. ATC does not separate IFR from VFR in Class E — tests knowledge of airspace-dependent separation responsibility. Generation rule: straight-line southbound constant-altitude tracks from stated positions.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 12:45:00 UTC. RVSM airspace (FL290-FL410).

Aircraft 1 — BAW001: Position 51.000°N 020.000°W, Altitude FL370, Heading 270, Speed 490 kts
Aircraft 2 — AAL100: Position 51.000°N 019.980°W, Altitude FL370, Heading 090, Speed 510 kts

Both aircraft at same altitude and latitude, on opposite headings in RVSM airspace (North Atlantic tracks).

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both flying straight-line constant-altitude reciprocal tracks over the North Atlantic.

Identify all hazards and calculate time to minimum separation.
gold_decision:
CRITICAL HAZARD: LOSS OF SEPARATION IMMINENT.

Horizontal separation: Both at 51.000°N. Longitude difference = 020.000°W - 019.980°W = 0.020°. At 51°N, cos(51°) = 0.6293. Distance = 0.020° × 60 NM × 0.6293 = 0.755 NM. CURRENT SEPARATION IS 0.755 NM — FAR BELOW 5 NM EN-ROUTE MINIMUM.

Vertical: Both FL370 — ZERO vertical separation.

Closing rate: 490 + 510 = 1,000 kts (reciprocal headings). Time to 0 NM (passing point): 0.755 / 1,000 hours × 60 = 0.045 minutes = 2.7 seconds.

This is an ACTIVE LOSS OF SEPARATION and imminent collision scenario. Separation minimum (5 NM horizontal in NAT/oceanic, 1,000 ft vertical in RVSM) is violated in BOTH dimensions. TCAS RA will have already triggered. This is an Airprox event. If this is genuinely observed on radar, safety alerts to both aircraft must be issued within seconds.
required_safety_constraints: 0.755 NM horizontal + 0 ft vertical = complete loss of separation — collision risk
NAT RVSM minimum 1,000 ft vertical AND 5 NM (or applicable oceanic minimum) horizontal — both violated
TCAS RA must have activated on both aircraft — ATC must NOT issue conflicting instructions
Immediate Airprox report required per ICAO Annex 11 §2.26
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §4-6-1 — RVSM 1,000 ft vertical minimum
ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4.1 — 5 NM radar separation minimum
ICAO Annex 11 §2.26 — Airprox mandatory reporting

provenance.generation_rule: Two aircraft at FL370 on reciprocal tracks at same latitude 51°N. Lon gap 0.020° at 51°N = 0.020 × 60 × cos(51°) = 0.755 NM. Closing rate 1000 kts. This is an active loss-of-separation (0.755 NM < 5 NM) with zero vertical. The scenario probes whether an agent recognizes that 0.020° longitude is only 0.755 NM — a unit-conversion trap. Generation rule: straight-line constant-altitude reciprocal tracks at same latitude.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 09:00:00 UTC. Terminal approach environment.

Aircraft 1 — N1 (Super, A380): Position 33.935°N 118.405°W, Altitude 3,000 ft, Heading 250, Speed 160 kts (on ILS final approach)
Aircraft 2 — N2 (Large, B737): Position 33.927°N 118.345°W, Altitude 3,000 ft, Heading 250, Speed 155 kts (on ILS final approach, same runway)

Both on same ILS final. N2 is BEHIND N1 (N2 is further east, i.e., further from runway threshold).

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both flying straight-line constant-altitude ILS approach tracks toward runway 25L at KLAX.

Identify the wake turbulence hazard and calculate current separation.
gold_decision:
HAZARDS:
1. Wake turbulence separation check: N1 (Super/A380) leading, N2 (Large/B737) following. Per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4: Large following Super requires 6 NM separation on final approach.

2. Current horizontal separation: Both at same latitude approximately (33.935°N vs 33.927°N — small difference). Longitude difference: 118.405°W vs 118.345°W = 0.060°. At 33.93°N, cos(33.93°) ≈ 0.8303. Distance ≈ 0.060° × 60 × 0.8303 ≈ 2.99 NM.

Current separation ≈ 3.0 NM — this is BELOW the required 6 NM wake turbulence minimum for Large following Super on final approach.

Loss of wake turbulence separation: The B737 (Large) is only 3 NM behind the A380 (Super). Required is 6 NM. The controller must immediately issue a go-around to N2 or vector N2 to increase spacing before continuing the approach.
required_safety_constraints: 6 NM wake turbulence minimum required for Large following Super on final approach per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4
3 NM current separation is exactly the standard terminal minimum but only half the required wake turbulence minimum
A go-around or extended downwind must be issued immediately to N2 to restore the 6 NM wake turbulence standard
Super/A380 wake vortex is the most intense category — the 6 NM minimum is non-negotiable
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4 — Wake turbulence final approach separation (Large behind Super = 6 NM)
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-4-3 — Wake turbulence categories (Super: A380)

provenance.generation_rule: A380 (Super) leading B737 (Large) on ILS final. Lon gap 0.060° at 33.93°N = 0.060 × 60 × cos(33.93°) = 0.060 × 60 × 0.8303 = 2.99 NM. Wake turbulence minimum for Large following Super = 6 NM per JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4. 3 NM is below 6 NM — active wake turbulence separation violation. Trap: 3 NM is the standard terminal minimum, which agents may incorrectly accept as sufficient. Generation rule: straight-line ILS final tracks at constant altitude from stated positions.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 15:20:00 UTC. En-route airspace, CONUS, Class A.

Aircraft 1 — N100 (B777 Heavy): Position 39.000°N 105.000°W, FL380, Heading 090, Speed 500 kts
Aircraft 2 — N200 (B737 Large): Position 39.050°N 105.000°W, FL380, Heading 000 (northbound), Speed 430 kts

The aircraft are crossing tracks: N100 is heading east, N200 is heading north. Both at same longitude, different latitudes by 0.050°.

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): N100 flying straight-line eastbound. N200 flying straight-line northbound. Both at FL380 constant altitude.

Identify whether a loss-of-separation hazard exists or will develop.
gold_decision:
CURRENT SEPARATION:
Both aircraft at 105.000°W (same longitude). Latitude difference: 39.050°N - 39.000°N = 0.050°. Distance = 0.050° × 60 NM = 3.0 NM north-south. This is the current separation.

3.0 NM < 5 NM en-route minimum. BOTH AIRCRAFT ARE ALREADY BELOW THE 5 NM EN-ROUTE MINIMUM.

Vertical: Both at FL380 — ZERO vertical separation.

This is an ACTIVE LOSS OF SEPARATION. Both aircraft are at the same altitude (FL380) and only 3.0 NM apart. N100 is moving east, N200 is moving north — they are diverging (N100 goes east, N200 goes north), so the separation WILL increase. N100 will move away from N200's track as N100 moves east. However, since they are already within 5 NM at the same altitude, a safety alert is mandatory NOW, even if separation is increasing.

Additionally: N200 is NORTH of N100. As N100 moves east and N200 moves north, the geometric separation is increasing — but the violation ALREADY EXISTS at this snapshot time.
required_safety_constraints: 3.0 NM current horizontal separation < 5 NM en-route minimum — active loss-of-separation even though aircraft are diverging
Zero vertical separation at FL380 means horizontal separation is the only safeguard
Safety alert mandatory per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-6 even if tracks are diverging — the violation exists NOW
Mandatory occurrence reporting required for this event
acceptable_variants: Exact 3.0 NM calculated as straight latitude difference — haversine at this small distance is effectively the same
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 — 5 NM en-route separation minimum
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-6 — Safety alert (mandatory even if diverging)

provenance.generation_rule: Two crossing-track aircraft at FL380, same longitude 105°W. Lat diff 0.050° = 3.0 NM. Both at same altitude = zero vertical. N100 eastbound, N200 northbound — diverging geometrically, but 3 NM < 5 NM minimum means active violation NOW. Key trap: agents may note 'diverging tracks' and conclude no hazard — wrong, because the violation already exists at the snapshot time. Generation rule: straight-line constant-altitude tracks from stated positions/headings.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 11:00:00 UTC. Terminal environment, Class B airspace.

Aircraft 1 — AA301 (Large, B737): Position 40.640°N 073.780°W, Altitude 5,000 ft, Heading 130, Speed 200 kts (inbound to JFK ILS 31R)
Aircraft 2 — DL402 (Heavy, B767): Position 40.770°N 073.862°W, Altitude 5,000 ft, Heading 130, Speed 190 kts (inbound to JFK ILS 31L, parallel runway)

Runway 31L/31R at JFK: centerline spacing approximately 1,135 ft (close parallel runways).

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both on straight-line constant-altitude ILS approaches.

Identify all hazards in this scenario.
gold_decision:
CRITICAL HAZARDS:
1. PARALLEL RUNWAY SPACING: JFK 31L/31R have approximately 1,135 ft centerline spacing. This is WELL BELOW the 4,300 ft minimum required for simultaneous independent parallel approaches per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-6. JFK 31L/31R is a DEPENDENT parallel approach configuration — aircraft must be staggered by at least 1.5 NM diagonal separation.

2. Current separation: Lat diff = 40.770° - 40.640° = 0.130° = 7.8 NM. Lon diff = 073.862° - 073.780° = 0.082°. At 40.7°N, cos(40.7°) = 0.7581; lon distance = 0.082° × 60 × 0.7581 = 3.73 NM. Total = √(7.8² + 3.73²) = √(60.84 + 13.91) = √74.75 = 8.64 NM diagonal separation. This exceeds 1.5 NM dependent parallel requirement.

3. Both at 5,000 ft = zero vertical separation — horizontal-only.

4. DL402 (Heavy/B767) is on 31L, AA301 (Large/B737) on 31R. AA301 is the Large following Heavy on different runway — wake turbulence awareness required in wind conditions that could carry Heavy wake to 31R.

Current separation 8.64 NM diagonal is sufficient for dependent approaches. No immediate loss-of-separation. Monitor for compression on final.
required_safety_constraints: JFK 31L/31R close-parallel runways require DEPENDENT approach procedures — 1.5 NM diagonal separation minimum
Wake turbulence from Heavy (B767) on 31L can drift to 31R in crosswind — wind assessment required
1,135 ft centerline spacing precludes independent simultaneous approaches (minimum 4,300 ft required)
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-6 — Parallel approach requirements (4,300 ft for independent)
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-5 — Dependent simultaneous approaches (1.5 NM diagonal)
JFK airport diagram — 31L/31R runway spacing

provenance.generation_rule: JFK 31L/31R parallel approaches, close-parallel (1135 ft spacing). DL402 B767 Heavy on 31L, AA301 B737 Large on 31R. Positions: lat diff 0.130° = 7.8 NM, lon diff 0.082° at 40.7°N = 3.73 NM, diagonal 8.64 NM. Dependent parallel minimum 1.5 NM diagonal — currently met. Hazard is wake turbulence and parallel runway classification. Generation rule: straight-line constant-altitude ILS approach tracks from stated positions.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 20:00:00 UTC. En-route airspace.

Aircraft 1 — N501: Position 45.000°N 100.000°W, FL350, Heading 045, Speed 460 kts
Aircraft 2 — N602: Position 44.000°N 099.000°W, FL330, Heading 225, Speed 440 kts

The aircraft are on reciprocal diagonal tracks (N501 heading NE at 045°, N602 heading SW at 225°). Both converging toward a common intersection.

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both flying straight-line constant-altitude tracks.

Calculate approximate current horizontal separation. Determine if a conflict exists or will develop given the different altitudes.
gold_decision:
CURRENT SEPARATION:
Lat diff: 45.000° - 44.000° = 1.000° = 60.0 NM north-south component.
Lon diff: 100.000° - 099.000° = 1.000°. At avg latitude ~44.5°N, cos(44.5°) = 0.7133; lon distance = 1.000° × 60 × 0.7133 = 42.80 NM.
Total distance = √(60.0² + 42.80²) = √(3600 + 1831.8) = √5431.8 = 73.7 NM.

VERTICAL SEPARATION: FL350 - FL330 = 2,000 ft. In RVSM airspace (FL290-FL410), minimum is 1,000 ft. 2,000 ft > 1,000 ft. Vertical separation IS maintained.

CLOSING: Both aircraft heading toward each other (045° and 225° are exactly reciprocal). Closing rate = 460 + 440 = 900 kts. Current separation 73.7 NM / 900 kts = 0.082 hours = 4.9 minutes until they PASS EACH OTHER (at the intersection). But vertical separation (2,000 ft) is maintained throughout — no horizontal minimum applies as long as vertical >= 1,000 ft.

NO HAZARD: Vertical separation 2,000 ft ≥ 1,000 ft RVSM minimum. Aircraft may cross without horizontal separation requirement being applied.
required_safety_constraints: When vertical separation ≥ RVSM minimum (1,000 ft), horizontal separation is not required — vertical alone is sufficient
If either aircraft changes altitude to FL340, vertical separation drops to 1,000 ft — still compliant but at minimum
Monitor: if an altitude change brings either aircraft within 1,000 ft of the other, 5 NM horizontal must then be established
acceptable_variants: Exact diagonal distance via haversine: slightly different from Euclidean approximation; at 73+ NM the approximation is reasonable but haversine is more precise
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §4-6-1 — RVSM 1,000 ft vertical minimum (FL290-FL410)
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-1 — Vertical separation as complete separation method

provenance.generation_rule: Two crossing aircraft on reciprocal diagonal tracks (045 vs 225). N501 at FL350, N602 at FL330 = 2000 ft vertical separation. Current separation 73.7 NM (Euclidean approximation). Vertical 2000 ft ≥ 1000 ft RVSM minimum = NO hazard. Tests that agent understands vertical separation is SUFFICIENT alone — no horizontal minimum needed. Generation rule: straight-line constant-altitude diagonal tracks from stated positions.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 07:30:00 UTC. Approach control environment, Class C airspace.

Aircraft 1 — N111 (IFR, B737): Position 32.895°N 097.040°W, Altitude 3,000 ft, Heading 180, Speed 160 kts (cleared ILS approach RWY 18R)
Aircraft 2 — N222 (VFR, C172): Position 32.840°N 097.044°W, Altitude 2,500 ft, Heading 180, Speed 120 kts (in pattern, Class C airspace)

N111 is BEHIND N222 (N111 is further north, N222 is further south and slower).

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both flying straight-line southbound constant-altitude tracks.

Identify all hazards and ATC separation obligations.
gold_decision:
HAZARDS:
1. VERTICAL: N111 at 3,000 ft, N222 at 2,500 ft = 500 ft vertical. IFR-VFR in Class C: ATC does not provide IFR-VFR separation. However, ATC must ensure N111 (IFR) does not conflict — traffic advisory required.

2. HORIZONTAL: Lat diff = 32.895° - 32.840° = 0.055° = 3.3 NM (N111 is behind). Lon diff negligible. N111 is 3.3 NM behind and faster (160 vs 120 kts). Closing rate = 40 kts. Time to 0 NM: 3.3/40 × 60 = 4.95 minutes.

3. ATC OBLIGATION IN CLASS C: ATC provides sequencing of VFR, traffic advisories, and workload-permitting conflict resolution. ATC does NOT provide IFR-VFR separation in Class C — the pilot of N111 retains see-and-avoid responsibility for N222. However, a traffic advisory MUST be issued.

4. At 120 kts N222 vs 160 kts N111: in 4.95 minutes they will be co-located unless N222 lands or N111 slows. Controller should issue traffic advisory to N111 regarding N222 and may suggest speed reduction or vector.
required_safety_constraints: Class C: ATC separates IFR from IFR; VFR-IFR = traffic advisory only, not separation service
N111 (IFR) must maintain visual contact with N222 if relying on see-and-avoid
Controller should issue traffic advisory immediately given 3.3 NM separation and 40 kt closing rate (< 5 minutes to same position)
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-8-3 — Class C airspace services
FAA AIM §3-2-4 — Class C services (sequencing, not full separation)
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-22 — Traffic advisories

provenance.generation_rule: IFR B737 and VFR C172 in Class C approach environment. N111 behind N222 by 0.055° lat = 3.3 NM. Closing rate 40 kts, time to 0 NM = 4.95 min. Vertical 500 ft. Class C: no IFR-VFR separation — tests knowledge of airspace-dependent ATC obligation. Generation rule: straight-line southbound constant-altitude tracks from stated positions.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 14:10:00 UTC. En-route Class A airspace (RVSM).

Four aircraft at the same latitude band, all heading eastbound (090°):

N-A: Position 37.000°N 082.000°W, FL330, 480 kts
N-B: Position 37.000°N 081.925°W, FL330, 450 kts
N-C: Position 37.000°N 081.850°W, FL330, 450 kts
N-D: Position 37.000°N 081.775°W, FL330, 420 kts

All on same eastbound track at FL330. N-A is furthest west (last), N-D is furthest east (first).

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): All four aircraft flying straight-line eastbound constant-altitude tracks.

Identify any separation issues and calculate in-trail spacings.
gold_decision:
IN-TRAIL SEPARATIONS at 37°N (cos 37° = 0.7986):

N-A to N-B: 082.000° - 081.925° = 0.075° × 60 × 0.7986 = 3.59 NM. BELOW 5 NM minimum.
N-B to N-C: 081.925° - 081.850° = 0.075° × 60 × 0.7986 = 3.59 NM. BELOW 5 NM minimum.
N-C to N-D: 081.850° - 081.775° = 0.075° × 60 × 0.7986 = 3.59 NM. BELOW 5 NM minimum.

All three in-trail gaps are 3.59 NM — BELOW the 5 NM en-route minimum.

Additionally:
N-A (480 kts) is catching N-B (450 kts) at 30 kts closing rate.
N-B and N-C are at equal speed — constant 3.59 NM separation.
N-C (450 kts) is catching N-D (420 kts) at 30 kts closing rate.

MULTIPLE ACTIVE LOSSES OF SEPARATION. All three pairs are below 5 NM. Safety alerts required for each pair. This is a systemic flow-management problem — the entire stream is under-separated. Controller must immediately increase spacing for the entire flow.
required_safety_constraints: Three simultaneous active losses of separation — all pairs below 5 NM at FL330
N-A catching N-B and N-C catching N-D — separations for these pairs are actively decreasing
Systemic resolution required: reduce speed of leading aircraft or vector each aircraft to increase spacing
Multiple simultaneous safety alerts create high controller workload — supervisor notification required
acceptable_variants: Haversine distances at 37°N: 0.075° × 60 × cos(37°) = 0.075 × 60 × 0.7986 = 3.593 NM; accept ±0.1 NM
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 — 5 NM en-route separation minimum
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-6 — Safety alerts (required for each violation)

provenance.generation_rule: Four eastbound aircraft at FL330 at 37°N. Each 0.075° lon apart = 0.075 × 60 × cos(37°) = 3.59 NM. All three pairs below 5 NM minimum. N-A (480 kt) catching N-B (450 kt); N-C (450 kt) catching N-D (420 kt). Systemic under-separation scenario. Generation rule: straight-line eastbound constant-altitude tracks at stated positions/speeds.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 19:45:00 UTC. Terminal area, Class B airspace.

Aircraft 1 — UAL900 (Heavy, B777): Position 40.630°N 073.775°W, Altitude 2,500 ft, Heading 130, Speed 170 kts (on ILS 31L final at JFK)
Aircraft 2 — EJA200 (Small, Citation X): Position 40.670°N 073.832°W, Altitude 2,500 ft, Heading 130, Speed 165 kts (on ILS 31L final, same runway)

EJA200 is BEHIND UAL900 (EJA200 is further NW).

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both on straight-line constant-altitude ILS 31L final approach tracks.

Identify the wake turbulence hazard and separation sufficiency.
gold_decision:
WAKE TURBULENCE ANALYSIS:

Leading aircraft: UAL900 = B777 = Heavy category.
Following aircraft: EJA200 = Citation X = Small category.
Required separation per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4: Small behind Heavy on final = 6 NM.

Current separation:
Lat diff: 40.670° - 40.630° = 0.040° = 2.4 NM north component.
Lon diff: 073.832° - 073.775° = 0.057°. At 40.65°N, cos(40.65°) = 0.7586; lon dist = 0.057° × 60 × 0.7586 = 2.60 NM.
Total diagonal ≈ √(2.4² + 2.6²) = √(5.76 + 6.76) = √12.52 = 3.54 NM.

Current separation ≈ 3.54 NM. Required: 6 NM for Small following Heavy.

WAKE TURBULENCE SEPARATION VIOLATION: 3.54 NM << 6 NM required. Closing rate: 170 - 165 = 5 kts (barely closing). But the separation is ALREADY insufficient.

IMMEDIATE ACTION REQUIRED: Issue go-around to EJA200 or vector to a longer downwind. The small Citation X is at high risk from B777 wake vortex at 3.54 NM.
required_safety_constraints: Small following Heavy = 6 NM minimum per JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4 — 3.54 NM is a critical violation
B777 generates intense wake vortex — Small aircraft like Citation X are at maximum risk
Go-around or extended downwind is the only safe resolution — speed reduction alone is insufficient given the 6 NM gap required
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4 — Wake turbulence final approach separation (Small behind Heavy = 6 NM)
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-4-3 — Wake turbulence categories

provenance.generation_rule: B777 (Heavy) leading, Citation X (Small) following on ILS 31L. Positions at 40.630°N 073.775°W and 40.670°N 073.832°W. Lat diff 0.040° = 2.4 NM, lon diff 0.057° at 40.65°N = 2.60 NM, diagonal 3.54 NM. Required: Small behind Heavy = 6 NM. Active wake turbulence separation violation. Generation rule: straight-line constant-altitude ILS approach tracks from stated positions.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 13:00:00 UTC. En-route airspace, CONUS, FL270 (below RVSM band).

Aircraft 1 — SKW1: Position 36.000°N 115.000°W, FL270, Heading 090, Speed 410 kts
Aircraft 2 — SKW2: Position 36.100°N 115.000°W, FL270, Heading 090, Speed 410 kts

Both aircraft heading eastbound on parallel tracks separated by 0.100° of latitude. Both at FL270 (same altitude).

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both flying straight-line eastbound constant-altitude tracks, 0.100° of latitude apart.

Identify if separation exists and what type of separation is applicable.
gold_decision:
SEPARATION ANALYSIS:

Horizontal (lateral) separation: Both heading east. Lat difference = 0.100° = 6.0 NM. Both at same altitude (FL270) and same speed — they will maintain the constant 6.0 NM lateral separation as they both proceed east.

Vertical: FL270 both — ZERO vertical separation.

FL270 is below the RVSM band (FL290-FL410). Standard vertical separation minimum below FL290 is 1,000 ft.

Is 6.0 NM lateral separation sufficient? Yes. En-route radar separation minimum is 5 NM. 6.0 NM > 5 NM. Currently compliant.

However, if either aircraft deviates from track (weather, deviation), the lateral separation could decrease. Also, note that 0.100° of latitude = exactly 6.0 NM (straight latitude conversion) — this is a clean parallel track separation.

NO IMMEDIATE HAZARD. Separation is maintained: 6.0 NM lateral > 5 NM minimum. Monitor for track deviations.
required_safety_constraints: 6.0 NM lateral separation exceeds 5 NM minimum — currently compliant
Same altitude + same speed = no vertical separation and constant horizontal separation
Any lateral deviation toward the other aircraft reduces the 1 NM margin quickly — controller should be aware of the thin margin
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 — En-route 5 NM minimum
Latitude degree conversion: 1° latitude = 60 NM universally (lat deg are constant-length)

provenance.generation_rule: Two parallel eastbound aircraft at FL270, same speed 410 kt. Lat separation 0.100° = 6.0 NM (latitude degrees are always 60 NM per degree). 6.0 NM > 5 NM minimum. Zero vertical, thin horizontal margin. Tests correct calculation of lat-based distance and appropriate no-hazard conclusion. Generation rule: straight-line eastbound constant-altitude parallel tracks from stated positions.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 17:30:00 UTC. En-route, FL390 (RVSM airspace).

Aircraft 1 — CAL1: Position 48.000°N 070.000°W, FL390, Heading 090, Speed 500 kts
Aircraft 2 — CAL2: Position 48.000°N 069.880°W, Altitude FL390, Heading 090, Speed 500 kts

Both eastbound, same track, same altitude, same speed. CAL1 is BEHIND CAL2.

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both flying straight-line eastbound constant-altitude tracks.

Identify the separation status. Is this configuration sustainable?
gold_decision:
CURRENT SEPARATION:
Both at 48.000°N. Lon diff = 070.000° - 069.880° = 0.120°. At 48°N, cos(48°) = 0.6691. Distance = 0.120° × 60 × 0.6691 = 4.82 NM.

4.82 NM < 5 NM en-route minimum. ACTIVE LOSS OF SEPARATION.

Vertical: Both FL390 — zero vertical separation. No vertical protection.

Same speed (500 kts both) — separation is CONSTANT at 4.82 NM. It will not decrease further, but it also will not increase. The separation is consistently below the 5 NM minimum.

SUSTAINABILITY: This configuration is NOT sustainable because it violates the 5 NM minimum. The fact that separation is not worsening does not make it acceptable — it is already below the minimum and remains below. ATC must immediately issue a speed change (slow CAL2 to let CAL1 fall further behind, or descend/climb one aircraft to apply vertical separation).

A safety alert must be issued. Supervisor must be notified. Occurrence report required.
required_safety_constraints: 4.82 NM < 5 NM en-route minimum — active violation even though constant (not worsening)
Same speed means separation will not naturally increase — active ATC intervention required
Vertical separation cannot save this — both at FL390 = zero vertical
Occurrence reporting mandatory even if no collision results
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 — 5 NM en-route minimum
ICAO Annex 11 §2.26 — Mandatory reporting of separation infringements

provenance.generation_rule: Two eastbound aircraft at FL390, same speed 500 kt. Lon gap 0.120° at 48°N = 0.120 × 60 × cos(48°) = 0.120 × 60 × 0.6691 = 4.82 NM. Constant separation (same speed) below 5 NM minimum. Key trap: agents may reason 'constant, not worsening' = acceptable. Wrong: violation persists. Generation rule: straight-line eastbound constant-altitude tracks from stated positions.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 06:00:00 UTC. Departure environment, Class C airspace, tower-controlled airport.

Aircraft 1 — N-DEP1 (B737 Large): Airborne 30 seconds ago, climbing through 2,000 ft, Heading 090, Speed 200 kts climbing, departed RWY 09R.
Aircraft 2 — N-DEP2 (A380 Super): Airborne 90 seconds ago, climbing through 4,500 ft, Heading 090, Speed 210 kts climbing, departed RWY 09L (parallel runway, 750 ft centerline separation).

N-DEP2 departed first and is ahead and higher. N-DEP1 departed 60 seconds later.

Estimated positions from departure end of runway (SYNTHETIC: straight-line climb from runway threshold at stated heading/speed):
N-DEP1: departed RWY 09R threshold at 34.200°N 118.350°W, now 30 sec × (200/3600) hr × 60 NM ≈ 1.67 NM from threshold: 34.200°N 118.321°W (estimated)
N-DEP2: departed RWY 09L threshold at 34.214°N 118.350°W (750 ft = 0.123 NM north), now 90 sec × (210/3600) × 60 ≈ 5.25 NM from threshold: 34.214°N 118.262°W (estimated)

Identify all hazards.
gold_decision:
HAZARDS:
1. WAKE TURBULENCE (Critical): N-DEP2 is an A380 (Super) that departed 60 seconds before N-DEP1 (B737 Large). Wake turbulence departure separation for Large following Super is 3 minutes (180 seconds) from the Super's brake release per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-4-3f. Only 90 seconds have elapsed since N-DEP2 departed — N-DEP1 should NOT have been released.

2. SAME DEPARTURE HEADING: Both heading 090°. N-DEP2 is ahead by 5.25 - 1.67 = 3.58 NM (estimated). Both at different altitudes: N-DEP1 at 2,000 ft, N-DEP2 at 4,500 ft = 2,500 ft vertical separation. Vertical OK.

3. HORIZONTAL: Lateral offset from parallel runways: 750 ft (0.123 NM) — essentially same track after departure. At current distances, separation appears to be maintained horizontally (N-DEP2 is 3.58 NM further east).

PRIMARY HAZARD: Wake turbulence departure timing. N-DEP1 was released only 60 seconds after N-DEP2 (Super). Required: 180 seconds. This is a potential regulatory violation and safety risk.
required_safety_constraints: Wake turbulence departure minimum after Super departure: 3 minutes (180 seconds) for following Large — only 90 seconds elapsed, violation exists
The 750 ft runway separation makes the parallel runways essentially the same departure path for wake turbulence purposes
N-DEP2 (A380 Super) generates the most intense wake vortex category — 3-minute wait is non-negotiable
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-4-3f — Wake turbulence departure separation (Super preceding)
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z Table 7-4-3 — Wake turbulence departure time minima

provenance.generation_rule: Departure wake turbulence scenario. A380 Super departs 90 sec before B737 Large on close-parallel runways (750 ft). Required: 3 minutes after Super departure per JO 7110.65Z §7-4-3f. Position estimates: N-DEP1 at 30 sec × (200/3600) × 60 = 1.67 NM from threshold; N-DEP2 at 90 sec × (210/3600) × 60 = 5.25 NM. Vertical 2500 ft OK; wake turbulence timing violated. Generation rule: straight-line climb from threshold at stated heading/speed/time.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 23:00:00 UTC. RVSM airspace, FL350.

Aircraft 1 — NAX1: Position 60.000°N 010.000°W, FL350, Heading 270, Speed 480 kts (westbound)
Aircraft 2 — NAX2: Position 60.500°N 010.000°W, FL350, Heading 090, Speed 470 kts (eastbound)

Both aircraft at the same longitude, different latitudes by 0.500°. Both at same altitude FL350. NAX1 heading west, NAX2 heading east.

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both flying straight-line constant-altitude tracks.

Identify the separation status and determine if a future conflict will develop.
gold_decision:
CURRENT SEPARATION:
Both at 010.000°W (same longitude). Lat diff = 60.500° - 60.000° = 0.500°. Latitude distance = 0.500° × 60 NM = 30.0 NM.

Vertical: Both FL350 — zero vertical separation.

FUTURE CONFLICT: NAX1 heading 270 (west) and NAX2 heading 090 (east) — they are moving APART laterally (one going west, one going east). Their tracks are parallel but they are moving in opposite directions. The north-south separation (30.0 NM) remains constant as they diverge east-west. No crossing occurs.

WILL THEY CONFLICT? No. Both aircraft maintain 30 NM lateral separation (north-south) as they each proceed on their respective east/west headings. The lateral component (30 NM) does not change. This exceeds the 5 NM en-route minimum.

NO HAZARD: Current separation 30 NM >> 5 NM minimum. Future tracks diverge — no conflict will develop. Zero vertical separation is noted but irrelevant given 30 NM horizontal separation.
required_safety_constraints: 30 NM lateral separation far exceeds 5 NM en-route minimum — no action required
Zero vertical: if either aircraft changes to the other's latitude track, separation must be re-evaluated
The east-west divergence means this pair's separation only increases over time — no monitoring concern
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 — 5 NM en-route minimum
Trajectory analysis: perpendicular tracks that do not converge maintain constant lateral separation

provenance.generation_rule: Two aircraft at 60°N, same longitude, 0.500° lat apart (30 NM). NAX1 westbound, NAX2 eastbound. Parallel tracks (both E-W) but offset 30 NM N-S — they diverge and never converge. Zero vertical. No hazard. Tests whether agent correctly identifies that E-W diverging tracks cannot conflict even with zero vertical. Generation rule: straight-line constant-altitude tracks from stated positions.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 10:00:00 UTC. En-route Class A, RVSM airspace.

Aircraft 1 — SKD1: Position 33.000°N 117.000°W, FL310, Heading 000, Speed 440 kts (northbound)
Aircraft 2 — SKD2: Position 36.000°N 117.000°W, FL310, Heading 180, Speed 460 kts (southbound)

Both aircraft on same longitude, converging on same track (one northbound, one southbound), same altitude.

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both flying straight-line constant-altitude tracks.

Calculate time to minimum separation (0 NM = head-on pass) and determine the time to 5 NM separation.
gold_decision:
CURRENT SEPARATION:
Lat diff: 36.000° - 33.000° = 3.000° = 180.0 NM north-south separation.

Both at FL310, zero vertical separation. Reciprocal headings (000 vs 180).
Closing rate: 440 + 460 = 900 kts.

Time to 5 NM separation: (180.0 - 5.0) NM / 900 kts = 175.0 / 900 = 0.1944 hours = 11.67 minutes.
Time to 0 NM (head-on): 180.0 / 900 = 0.2 hours = 12.0 minutes.

HAZARD: These aircraft will reach 5 NM in 11.67 minutes and will be head-on at 12.0 minutes without intervention. Currently 180 NM apart — no immediate violation. However, controller MUST act now to prevent the conflict. Options: climb or descend one aircraft to provide 1,000 ft vertical separation (fastest), or vector one aircraft off the track.

With 11.67 minutes to the 5 NM threshold, the controller has time but must act promptly. Standard practice: issue altitude change or vector at least 5-10 minutes before projected conflict.
required_safety_constraints: 11.67 minutes to 5 NM separation — controller must issue resolution within the next 5 minutes to allow pilot execution time
180 NM current separation is adequate — this is an advance conflict prediction, not an active violation
Preferred resolution: vertical separation (fastest — immediate altitude change), then vector if vertical not available
acceptable_variants: Exact calculation: 175 NM / 900 kt = 11.667 min to 5 NM threshold; accept ±0.2 min
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 — 5 NM en-route minimum
Time to conflict = (current separation - minimum) / closing rate

provenance.generation_rule: Northbound (440 kt) and southbound (460 kt) aircraft at FL310 on same longitude. Current sep 3.000° lat = 180 NM. Closing rate 900 kt. Time to 5 NM: 175/900 × 60 = 11.67 min. No active violation; advance conflict prediction. Tests proactive conflict prediction — agents must compute time-to-conflict and recommend resolution before the violation occurs. Generation rule: straight-line constant-altitude reciprocal tracks from stated positions.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 22:00:00 UTC. En-route, Class A airspace.

Aircraft 1 — TRS1: Position 42.000°N 080.000°W, FL290, Heading 045, Speed 420 kts
Aircraft 2 — TRS2: Position 42.000°N 080.000°W, FL290, Heading 135, Speed 430 kts

Both aircraft at EXACTLY the same position (same lat, lon, altitude).

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both at the same point at this snapshot time, diverging on tracks 045° and 135°.

Identify the hazard severity and required ATC action.
gold_decision:
CRITICAL HAZARD: BOTH AIRCRAFT AT IDENTICAL COORDINATES AND ALTITUDE.

Horizontal separation: 0.000 NM — complete loss of separation.
Vertical separation: 0 ft — complete loss of separation.

This is an active airprox / potential collision event. TCAS RA must have triggered on both aircraft. ATC must:
1. Immediately acknowledge and assist if communication is established.
2. NOT issue conflicting instructions to TCAS RA maneuvers.
3. Alert supervisor and activate emergency response.
4. Issue safety alert to all other traffic in the area.
5. File mandatory occurrence report.

NOTE: The diverging headings (045° and 135°) mean they are moving away from each other, which reduces the risk of sustained conflict. However, at the instant of this snapshot, they are co-located — this IS a loss of separation event regardless of future divergence.

This scenario also requires investigation: how did two aircraft end up at the same position and altitude? Possible causes: radar track fusion error, actual collision, or data corruption.
required_safety_constraints: 0 NM horizontal and 0 ft vertical = complete simultaneous loss of all separation — most severe category
TCAS RA mandatory compliance by both pilots — ATC must not issue conflicting clearances
Supervisor notification and emergency response activation mandatory
Occurrence report and safety investigation mandatory
acceptable_variants: This scenario may represent a radar data error (track fusion) — ATC should also verify data integrity before concluding an actual collision has occurred
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §10-2-1 — Emergency procedures
ICAO Annex 11 §2.26 — Mandatory reporting of airprox/collision risk
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-29 — TCAS RA procedures

provenance.generation_rule: Two aircraft at exactly the same coordinates and altitude — worst-case loss-of-separation scenario. Diverging tracks (045° vs 135°) mitigate future risk but do not change the snapshot-time violation. Tests agent response to most extreme separation failure. Generation rule: point-in-space coincidence at stated position/altitude; both on straight-line constant-altitude diverging tracks from stated headings.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 16:30:00 UTC. Terminal departure environment, Class B airspace.

Aircraft 1 — N-REG1 (RVSM-approved B737): Position 33.941°N 118.408°W, climbing through FL200, Heading 250, Speed 280 kts
Aircraft 2 — N-REG2 (NON-RVSM C-130, military): Position 33.940°N 118.410°W, FL190, Heading 270, Speed 260 kts (level)

N-REG2 is a non-RVSM aircraft operating in RVSM airspace without authorization.

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): N-REG1 climbing through FL200 on heading 250. N-REG2 level at FL190 heading 270.

Identify all hazards including the non-RVSM issue.
gold_decision:
HAZARDS:
1. NON-RVSM AIRCRAFT IN RVSM AIRSPACE (Critical): N-REG2 (C-130) is non-RVSM at FL190. FL190 is below the RVSM band (FL290-FL410) — but if N-REG2 climbs into RVSM airspace, it would be non-RVSM in RVSM airspace. WAIT: FL190 is below FL290, so N-REG2 is NOT in RVSM airspace. Standard 1,000 ft vertical minimum below FL290 applies.

2. CURRENT SEPARATION: Lat diff = 33.941° - 33.940° = 0.001° = 0.06 NM. Lon diff = 118.410° - 118.408° = 0.002°. At 33.94°N, cos(33.94°) = 0.8302; lon dist = 0.002° × 60 × 0.8302 = 0.10 NM. Total ≈ 0.12 NM horizontal separation.

3. VERTICAL: N-REG1 climbing through FL200, N-REG2 at FL190 = 1,000 ft separation (below FL290, standard 1,000 ft minimum). AT MINIMUM.

4. HAZARD: N-REG1 is climbing through FL200 and will reach FL190 (downward) — wait, N-REG1 is at FL200 climbing, N-REG2 is at FL190 level. N-REG1 is 1,000 ft ABOVE N-REG2 and climbing away. Vertical separation is INCREASING. Horizontal 0.12 NM is extremely close but since vertical is 1,000 ft (exactly at minimum) and increasing, technically it is compliant but barely. The 0.12 NM horizontal with 1,000 ft vertical at standard airspace is the absolute minimum and concerning.
required_safety_constraints: Vertical exactly at 1,000 ft minimum below FL290 — compliant but at minimum; any climb exceedance by N-REG1 does not apply (it's climbing away)
0.12 NM horizontal separation is dangerously close — while vertical separates, any navigation error could cause loss
Non-RVSM C-130 at FL190 is BELOW RVSM band — not an RVSM violation, but monitoring for any climb into FL290+ required
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §4-6-1 — RVSM band FL290-FL410; below FL290 is standard 1,000 ft
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-1 — 1,000 ft vertical separation standard

provenance.generation_rule: B737 climbing through FL200, C-130 (non-RVSM) level at FL190. Both at KLAX-area coordinates — essentially co-located (0.12 NM horizontal). Vertical: 1000 ft exactly at below-FL290 standard minimum. N-REG1 climbing away = separation increasing. Key trap: non-RVSM label is a distractor (FL190 is below RVSM band). Generation rule: N-REG1 on straight-line constant-altitude climb trajectory on heading 250 from stated position; N-REG2 level constant-altitude on heading 270.

prompt:
Radar snapshot at 18:45:00 UTC. En-route, RVSM airspace.

Aircraft 1 — OAL1: Position 55.000°N 025.000°W, FL370, Heading 270, Speed 500 kts
Aircraft 2 — OAL2: Position 55.050°N 025.100°W, FL370, Heading 270, Speed 500 kts

Both aircraft heading the same direction (westbound), same altitude, same speed.

Trajectory (SYNTHETIC): Both flying straight-line westbound constant-altitude tracks, parallel offset.

Identify separation status and determine if this is an acceptable configuration.
gold_decision:
CURRENT SEPARATION:
Lat diff: 55.050° - 55.000° = 0.050° = 3.0 NM (north-south).
Lon diff: 025.100° - 025.000° = 0.100°. At 55.025°N avg, cos(55.025°) = 0.5730. Lon dist = 0.100° × 60 × 0.5730 = 3.44 NM.
Total diagonal = √(3.0² + 3.44²) = √(9.0 + 11.83) = √20.83 = 4.56 NM.

Vertical: Both FL370 — zero vertical separation.

Is 4.56 NM sufficient? NO. En-route radar minimum is 5 NM. 4.56 NM < 5 NM.

Both at same speed heading same direction — separation is CONSTANT at 4.56 NM. This will not improve without ATC intervention.

ACTIVE LOSS OF SEPARATION: 4.56 NM < 5 NM. ATC must intervene: alter heading, change altitude, or change speed of one aircraft to increase separation to ≥ 5 NM. Preferred fastest fix: assign one aircraft to FL380 or FL360 (1,000 ft vertical) — immediately resolves with vertical separation.
required_safety_constraints: 4.56 NM < 5 NM en-route minimum — active loss of separation
Same speed/heading means no natural improvement — ATC must actively resolve
Fastest fix: 1,000 ft altitude change to one aircraft provides immediate vertical separation
acceptable_variants: Haversine for these coordinates: cos(55.025°) ≈ 0.5730; total diagonal ≈ 4.56 NM; accept ±0.1 NM
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 — 5 NM en-route minimum
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §4-6-1 — 1,000 ft RVSM vertical as alternative separation

provenance.generation_rule: Two westbound aircraft at FL370, same speed. Lat diff 0.050° = 3.0 NM; lon diff 0.100° at 55°N = 0.100 × 60 × cos(55.025°) = 0.100 × 60 × 0.5730 = 3.44 NM; diagonal 4.56 NM. 4.56 < 5 NM. Constant separation (same speed/direction). Active violation. Tests calculation at high latitude (cos factor significant). Generation rule: straight-line westbound constant-altitude parallel tracks from stated positions.
